title: Physical completion of the Navier-Stokes equations
abstract: The incompressible Navier-Stokes equations contain viscous dissipation but no thermal noise. I show, using a topological argument based on Poincaré's lemma, that the fluctuation-dissipation relation for the full nonlinear dynamics can be derived without the linearisation or structural assumptions that all previous derivations require. The nonlinear convective term is Hamiltonian (energy-preserving and phase-space-volume-preserving) and drops out of the Fokker-Planck equilibrium condition exactly, so the noise derived from linearised fluctuations near equilibrium is in fact exact for the full nonlinear system. This result proves, rather than assumes, the reversible/irreversible decomposition that the GENERIC framework postulates, provided Poincaré's lemma holds on the phase space. The resulting stochastic system, with a physical molecular-scale spectral cutoff, is trivially globally well-posed: a finite-dimensional stochastic differential equation with non-degenerate noise and a confining Lyapunov function. It has a unique Gibbs equilibrium and converges to it exponentially. The difficulty of the Clay Millennium Prize Problem arises entirely from two idealisations, zero temperature and infinite spectral resolution, neither of which is satisfied by any physical fluid.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous derivation of the stochastic completion of the Navier-Stokes equations, showing that thermal noise is required to balance viscosity. It proves that by including a physical spectral cutoff, the system becomes globally well-posed and avoids the singularities predicted by the idealized, zero-temperature version of the equations.
main_problem: Addressing the physical incompleteness of the deterministic Navier-Stokes equations, specifically the lack of thermal noise to balance viscous dissipation and the potential for unphysical singularities.
main_result: The author proves that the fluctuation-dissipation relation is exact for the full nonlinear system and that a spectrally truncated stochastic Navier-Stokes system is globally well-posed with a unique Gibbs equilibrium.
method: A topological argument using Poincaré's lemma on a contractible Eulerian phase space to derive the fluctuation-dissipation relation without linearization.
model_or_system: A finite-dimensional, incompressible, viscous fluid at finite temperature modeled via stochastic differential equations on a three-torus with a molecular-scale spectral cutoff.
key_observables: Energy (E), probability distribution (P), and the diffusion matrix (D)
important_parameters: Viscosity (nu), temperature (T), density (rho), volume (V), and spectral cutoff (k_max)
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes a contractible phase space (e.g., the Eulerian phase space on a torus) and the existence of a Boltzmann equilibrium.
paper_structure: The paper begins by identifying the inconsistency in deterministic Navier-Stokes, introduces a stochastic model with a spectral cutoff, applies Poincaré's lemma to derive the fluctuation-dissipation relation, demonstrates the Hamiltonian nature of the convective term, and concludes by proving global well-posedness and discussing the implications for the Clay Millennium Problem.
why_interesting: The derivation of exact fluctuation-dissipation relations without linearization and the use of topological arguments to ensure stability are highly relevant to the study of non-equilibrium dynamics and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
